narrative_coherence.notes: Topics flow logically: 3.1 establishes the three AI eras (symbolic AI → ML → deep learning) and ends by explicitly pointing forward to LLMs (topic 3.2) and agents. 3.2 builds directly on 3.1's deep-learning foundation, traces the LLM milestone chain (word embeddings → Transformer → BERT/GPT → scale), and explicitly defers the attention mechanism to 3.4. 3.3 treats 3.1 and 3.2 as complete prerequisites and moves cleanly to the agent abstraction layer. Each topic opens by naming its dependency on the prior topic(s). The arc — rules → learned patterns → language models → autonomous agents — is historically coherent and pedagogically progressive. One soft issue: 3.3 corpus prerequisites section mislabels topic 3.1 as 'The Rise of Modern AI' rather than its actual title 'History of AI — Symbolic AI to Machine Learning to Deep Learning'. This is a drafting artifact in the corpus that does not affect the reading artifact or learning flow.

violations.finding: Topic 3.1 is cited as '3.1 — The Rise of Modern AI', which does not match its canonical title '3.1 — History of AI — symbolic AI to machine learning to deep learning'. Title drift in a prerequisite citation. Does not affect the reading artifact.
violations.recommendation: Correct the prerequisite title in 3.3 topic_corpus.md to match the canonical topic title.

violations.finding: 'Context window' is referenced as 'the context window you met in 3.2' (corpus) and used without inline definition in reading ('held in the LLM's context window'). The term does not appear in 3.2's vocabulary table or reading key-concepts section. The 3.3 reading partially mitigates this by describing context window inline ('everything the agent has seen so far in the current run, held in the LLM's context window'), but the back-reference to 3.2 is inaccurate.
violations.recommendation: Either add 'context window' to 3.2's vocabulary table, or remove the 'you met in 3.2' back-reference and rely solely on the 3.3 inline description.

violations.finding: The reading states '(The mathematical details of how a list of numbers represents meaning are formally covered in topic 6.3.)' Topic 6.3 is in a different module/course. This is a valid forward-reference and does not teach the out-of-scope material here — it correctly defers it. Flagged for awareness only; the handling is correct.
violations.recommendation: No action required. Forward-deferral is appropriate.

violations.finding: Transformer architecture is listed in the milestones table with a note '(see topic 3.4)' and is correctly deferred — not taught. In the reading.md the same milestone is mentioned only by name and year. Consistent deferral. No drift.

scope_coherence.notes: The three topics collectively cover all five elements of the module narrative seed: (1) History symbolic → ML → deep learning — covered in 3.1 in full. (2) How LLMs work at a high level (tokens, training, inference, parameters) — covered in 3.2 (parameters and training explicitly; tokens/inference not named as vocabulary but the conceptual equivalent is covered via the 'large model trained on text' definition and pre-train/fine-tune pattern). (3) AI in India — covered in 3.1 (Section 5.5) and 3.2 (Section 7, vernacular language processing). (4) The jagged frontier — introduced in 3.1 Section 7, mentioned in 3.2, with depth explicitly deferred to topic 3.8. (5) AI agents — covered in 3.3. One genuine gap: 'Why the same question gives different answers — temperature and sampling' appears in the module narrative seed but is not addressed in any of the three topic corpora or readings. This concept is not deferred to a named later topic; it is simply absent. This is a minor scope gap — it should be reviewed for coverage in the topic closest to it in the sequence (likely 3.8 or a later topic in the module if one exists). The gap does not fail the module because the five checks in the skill definition do not require complete narrative-seed coverage; it is surfaced here as an advisory.
scope_coherence.advisory: Temperature and sampling — referenced in module narrative seed but absent from all three topic artifacts. Confirm this concept is covered in a subsequent topic within M2 (e.g., topic 3.8 which is referenced in both 3.2 and the jagged-frontier thread).

no_duplicated_core_concepts.notes: Core-concept noun-set Jaccard similarity scores (estimated from vocabulary tables and defined terms): 3.1 vs 3.2 — approximately 0.10 (shared context: 'parameters', 'deep learning', 'training data' as pre-established terms, not re-defined). 3.2 vs 3.3 — approximately 0.12 (shared: 'LLM', 'hallucination', 'prompt', 'Transformer' all treated as established in 3.2 and referenced without re-definition in 3.3). 3.1 vs 3.3 — approximately 0.05. All pairs well below the 0.6 flag threshold. 'Hallucination' appears in 3.2 (defined) and 3.3 (referenced as a known term in prerequisites) — this is correct concept reuse, not duplication.

lab_activity_alignment.notes: Module lab seed: 'Capability Probe — test Claude across 5 task types: creative, factual, logical, ethical, coding / Document what worked, what failed, where it hallucinated / Journal Entry #2.' All three topics support this. 3.1 Section 9 provides a timeline reconstruction exercise and journal reflection prompt that feeds into Journal Entry 2. 3.2 Section 9 defines the Capability Probe exercise in full, listing all five task types with concrete example prompts, and explicitly links to Journal Entry 2 and Assessment 1. 3.3 Section 9 extends the Capability Probe with a sixth 'agent-mode simulation' dimension that maps to the 3.3 content. Lab coverage is thorough and well-distributed across topics.

audience_level_consistency.notes: All three topics maintain the absolute-beginner register consistently. 3.1 uses relatable analogies throughout (dog recognition, spam filter, elaborate checklist). 3.2 correctly builds on 3.1 vocabulary without re-teaching it and defers mathematical depth (embeddings, attention) to later topics. 3.3 maintains the same register and correctly treats 3.1 and 3.2 terms as established. No topic shows a register spike or assumes knowledge not introduced in a prior topic, with the one exception noted under concept_consistency (context window back-reference). Depth profiles are all 'standard' per manifests — consistent.

findings_summary.short: 'context window' back-referenced to 3.2 but not defined in 3.2 artifacts
'Temperature and sampling' in module narrative seed but absent from all three topic artifacts

verdict: PASS. The module forms a coherent historical arc from symbolic AI through machine learning and deep learning (3.1), to the emergence of large language models via the Transformer (3.2), to the agent abstraction layer (3.3). Terminology is introduced in sequence and reused consistently. No cross-module concept drift detected. Core concepts are distinct across all three topics (all Jaccard pairs below 0.15). The lab activity (Capability Probe + Journal Entry 2) is fully supported by topic artifacts. Three soft/advisory findings are raised for the author's awareness but none block module passage: a title mismatch in 3.3's corpus prerequisite list, a context-window back-reference without a corresponding 3.2 definition, and a missing temperature/sampling thread from the module narrative seed.
